explanation_md: A default-constructed `std::vector` is empty. `.size()` returns `0` (as a `std::size_t`). Even though `std::size_t` is unsigned, the value `0` prints normally as `0`.

options.a: The loop increments instead of decrementing
options.b: An unsigned variable can never be less than 0, so the condition is always true and the loop never stops
options.c: Accessing `v[i]` with a `std::size_t` index is undefined behavior
options.d: The initial value `v.size() - 1` will overflow because `v` is empty

explanation_md: Because `std::size_t` is unsigned, the condition `i >= 0` is always true — an unsigned integer can never hold a negative value. When `i` is `0` and we execute `--i`, it wraps around to the maximum value of `std::size_t`, causing an infinite loop with out-of-bounds access.

prompt_md: Which of these is the safest way to write a forward loop over a `std::vector<int> v` using an integer index?
explanation_md: Option c uses `std::ssize(v)` which returns a signed integer, so the comparison is signed vs signed — no implicit conversion, no wrapping risk. Option a compares signed `int` to unsigned `size_t`, generating a compiler warning. Option b uses `std::size_t` which is fine for forward loops but causes the infinite-loop bug if you ever decrement the counter. Option d has the same wrapping risk as option b.

options.a: The function should return by reference, not by value
options.b: `v.size()` is one past the last valid index; should be `v.size() - 1`
options.c: `std::size_t` cannot be used as an array index
options.d: The vector parameter must be passed by value for subscript to work

explanation_md: For a vector of size N, valid indices are 0 through N−1. `v.size()` equals N, which is one past the last valid index. Accessing `v[v.size()]` is undefined behavior. The correct expression is `v[v.size() - 1]`.

`v.size()` returns 4, cast to `int` as 4. `len - 1` is 3, which is the last valid index. `v[3]` is 40. This demonstrates the safe pattern: cast `.size()` to `int`, then use the signed `len - 1` without any underflow risk.
